Hi Mike - I just went over this whole thing, and it looks good to me!

Tests pass on both of my systems.

@craiglagegit I made some edits to the code to make it match GalSim style a bit more.  Things like 100 character lines, how we format the default values in doc strings.  That kind of thing.  

I also moved the stuff you had in `examples/brighter-fatter` to `devel/brighter-fatter`.  I think we'll want to make a single example for people to see how to use the new sensor stuff (maybe as part of the existing `lsst.py` example), but there was a bit too much in that directory for it to be useful as an example to end users, so `devel` seems like a better home for that.  But if you think there is something specific in there that would serve as a good example code for people to look at, let me know, and we can move it back.

In the C++ layer, I also took the opportunity to switch over the Polygon implementation to use std::vector rather than bare pointers and arrays, so no more new and delete calls anywhere.  I don't think you had any memory leaks, but it always feels a bit nicer to let the std library handle all that, so you don't have to worry about it.  This also ended up speeding things up significantly (roughly x2), so that's a nice bonus.  I made a few further tweaks, so the slow unit test, `test_bf_slopes`, which used to take 6.3 seconds on my laptop is now down to 2.6 seconds.

I have some ideas about how to optimize a bit further, but I haven't tried to code it up yet.  In particular, `insidePixel` is still a pretty tall pole.  I think we can use the fact that the Polygons are always nearly square to keep track of an inscribed and circumscribed square that will tell us whether a point is in or out pretty accurately most of the time faster than the current function does it (with the crossing number algorithm).  Then if the point is between the two squares, revert back to the current algorithm.

Another thought I had was to transpose the loops in `updatePixelDistortions` (another fairly tall pole, unsurprisingly).  The inner loop has a check if the pixel flux < 100.  If that happens pretty often, it might be faster to swap them so the `continue` happens in the outer loop instead.  I'm not sure how common this branch happens though, so it might not actually help much in practice.

Anyway, feel free to take a look at my changes.  I also have some specific line comments and questions that I'll try to get to this week.

> In my case, I am using a custom bootstrap method which is restoring a physical backup (created with pg_basebackup), performing a pg_upgrade on the backup and then subscribing a replica via the default pg_basebackup subscription.

It looks like very complicated setup. I would like to see your patroni.yaml and scripts for custom bootstrap and `post_bootstrap/post_init`.

But I can guess what is going on: after a custom bootstrap script has been executed patroni starts postgres and later updates initialize key with the value of `Database system identifier` (of the original cluster). In order to avoid running `pg_controldata` all the time patroni caches value of cluster sysid in memory, because nobody replaces data directory in runtime.

pg_upgrade requires you to run `initdb`, which generates a new `Database system identifier`, dumps/restores schemas and copying (or relinking) datafiles to a new directory. Also pg_upgrade starts and stops original cluster. Basically after pg_upgrade you get TWO data directories, the first one is original and another - upgraded to the new version.

What happens next? Do you somehow rename directories in order to give the new one to Patroni? But this is absolutely new cluster with the new sysid. And moreover, it requires a new postgres binaries (from another major version).

The only possible way to do a major upgrade right now is:
1. stop patroni
2. pg_upgrade
3. update patroni yaml
4. remove initialize key from DCS or wipe complete cluster state from DCS. The second one could be achieved by running `patronictl remove <cluster-name>`
5. may be copy `patroni.dynamic.json` from old data dir to the new one (if you wiped cluster state on previous step). It will help you to retain some postgres parameters you set before.
6. start patroni

After doing some internal debugging, I realised that in python 3.5.1, django 1.10.1 (I haven't checked other combinations), `request.META['SERVER_PORT']` is an int and not a str:

```python
ipdb> request.META['SERVER_PORT']
7000
```

This appears to be in contradiction with the django docs (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/request-response/#django.http.HttpRequest.META) that state:

> SERVER_PORT – The port of the server (as a string).

So I'd say this looks like it may be a bug/backwards-incompatible change in Django or python (DJango probably .

Maybe, to be on the defensive side, the opbeat server should accept integer values for `SERVER_PORT` if they are valid port numbers.

@neubig I suppose you are doing the "manual" installation. If you do `pip install .` then you won't have to run `cmake` yourself, since `setup.py` already does everything on its own!

If you are doing the manual installation, you still need to run `setup.py` at the end to install the Python bindings (as before, but it's no longer in `build/python` but rather in the root directory). Note that you must give it the build directory path so it knows you already built DyNet and doesn't try to build it again (I updated this in the docs as part of the PR):
```
cd build/python
python ../../setup.py build --build-dir=.. install --user
```

Note that `setup.py` looks at the `EIGEN3_INCLUDE_DIR` environment variable to determine where to save eigen, and if already exists, then it leaves it as is. If you did the manual installation and specified `-DEIGEN3_INCLUDE_DIR` to `cmake`, you should also define it as an environment variable with the same value before running `setup.py` at the end, or else add it to the command line, e.g.:
```
python ../../setup.py EIGEN3_INCLUDE_DIR=/usr/local/include/eigen build --build-dir=.. install --user
```

The `filter1d_narrow ` is a 1D convolution method, which can be explained as follows:
```
import dynet as dy

# filter1d_narrow()
# usage: e = dy.filter1d_narrow(e1, e2)
#        e1: Expression of n x s
#        e2: Expression of n x d
# This function will calculate the convolution along each dimension.
# For example, 0.8 = 1*0 + 2*0.1 + 3*0.2 + 0*0.5
#              1.6 = 2*0 + 3*0.1 + 4*0.2 + 1*0.5
#              1.4 = 3*0 + 4*0.1 + 0*0.2 + 2*0.5
e_input = dy.inputTensor([[1,2,3], [2,3,4], [3,4,0], [0,1,2]])
e_filter = dy.inputTensor([[0], [0.1], [0.2], [0.5]])
e = dy.filter1d_narrow(e_input, e_filter)
print('filter1d_narrow', e.npvalue()) # expect [[0.8 1.6 1.4]]
```

@gamaanderson Agreed, the **MdvFile** class should reflect the structure of MDV files as closely as possible.  **MdvFile** has not be updated recently and could use a fresh set of eyes, thanks for taking this on!  Have you seen how the unpacking of structures is accomplished in the **SigmetFile** and **NEXRADLevel2File** classes with the tuple-of-tuples and ` _unpack_structure` function?  Following this layout might simplify dealing with the MDV specific structures.

There is a [**MdvDisplay**](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/generated/pyart.graph.MdvDisplay.html#pyart.graph.MdvDisplay) class which can be used to plot MdvFile objects in the same manner as the RadarDisplay class is used to plot Radar objects.  I've in favor of depreciating this object along with the other file specific Display classes, **RslDisplay** and **CFRadialDisplay**, rather than supporting them in the long run.  Offering a single procedure for plotting data is simpler and will be easier to maintain in the long run and I think the loss of functionality would be acceptable.  In addition adding a **Display** class for each supported file format will become cumbersome, the **Radar** abstraction should be used instead.  Anyone who wanted to plot data without generating a **Radar** object would still be able to but they would need to write their own code to do the plotting.  Implementing this depreciation would also be a good time to refactor some of the modules in the graph directory.

The function `storage.connection._get_pem_key` is **only used** by the function `storage.connection._get_signed_query_params` which in turn is **only used** by `storage.connection.Connection.generate_signed_url`.

The only other place we use "crypto" is in our regression tests, checking an MD5 hash against a stored file.

------

```python
with Transaction() as xact:

    foo()
```

and `foo()` could be raising, causing a `rollback()`, so it would be nice to check if `xact.succeeded`.

@dhermes , wat I usually to is limit the string that needs to be translated to 1000 chars:

    s = "Hallo ik kom uit Nederland"  # Dutch for 'Hello, I'm from the Netherlands'
    client.translate(s[:1000])

This works fine. The error occurs when I try to translate a longer Chinese text. I think the issue is that a Chinese characters converts to a string consisting of ASCII characters, where every Chinese characters converts to multiple ASCII characters. The 1000-character limitation is performed on the original string, and thus way longer in the query sent to Google. Limiting the `unquote`'d string on 1000 characters seems to work:

    # This can fail; a Chinese 100-character string becomes way longer when `unquote`'d:
    body = "chinese string here"[:1000]
    client.translate(body)

    # This works
    body = unquote(quote("chinese string here")[:1000])
    client.translate(body)

So I think the issue was that a 1000-character limited Chinese string converts to a string that exceeds the maximum limit for a URL. Hence the need to POST the string in the body instead of the URL.

Scattered thoughts on stuff for my own future benefit (feel free to comment, but just know these are raw).

Clients:

1. We should just drop the common base class and depend on google.auth.default() for both default credential and project ID detection.
2. We should add a mixin class for `from_service_account_file()` and `from_service_account_info()`, with a deprecated alias for `from_service_account_json`. This can be used by gapic clients as well.

Connection/JSONConnection:

1. Stop using inheritance for configuration.
1. We should try to just treat this the same we do gRPC channels in gapic clients. The clients should accept a `connection`  argument and construct their own if not provided.
1. Connections should be reduced to just a basic class with an `http` property and a `request` method and made public.
1. The `request` method should handle serialization, deserialization, exceptions, and injecting clientinfo headers.
1. Storage seems to be the only client that does special things with the connection, so the bulk of the existing layers of code should be moved into storage. It's special enough to warrant that.
1. Some classes write "Stub"-like classes (like `_HTTPVisionAPI`). This is fine and aligns with the plans above.

Regarding whether this is a blocker re use of hostnames: I don't think you can use hostnames as suggested, and hence remains a blocker for using v0.1.7 with the current emulator.

In [regression repo](https://github.com/tom-haines/gcd-dot-one-seven-regression/), have a look at the last test (copied here):

Even when using a hostname with the emulator (here hardwired hostname "docker-host" to `docker-machine ip dev` in /etc/hosts )

    mvn clean test -Dgcp_ip=docker-host

Will FAIL.  

But will PASS under 0.1.6

    mvn -f pom-0.1.6.xml clean test -Dgcp_ip=docker-host


example config:

````
$ docker-machine ip dev
172.16.10.157
$ cat /etc/hosts
172.16.10.157 docker-host
````

( I've mentioned "docker-host" here, but equally you could just run gcd.sh on any machine of course )

@aozarov we are looking for API compatibility with the AppEngine Datastore API to ease moving code from GAE to GCE.  We are also looking for performance improvements since the current implementation is not usable at scale.

@dhermes AFAIK, this has been working all along. The datastore statistics feature consists of a number of specially-named entities -- they can be read through the Cloud Datastore API the same as any other entity.

The meaning field for existing values does get set: [Value.meaning](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/datastore/v1beta3/entity.proto#L173). As long as you leave the meaning set as you received it in the v1beta3 API, you should be able to maintain compatibility with your App Engine application.

I can repro the 7GB mem usage.

I checked the mem usage of ExternContext object, in particular, mem usage of _id_to_obj, _obj_to_id and _handles. I use sys.getsizeof method.

It seems _id_to_obj and _obj_to_id both uses around 50MB, and _handles uses around 134MB.
Since sys.getsizeof can only return size of the object itself, not including the size of the referenced objects in the original objects field, I added up the size of referenced objects as well.

```
size = 0
for k in self.scheduler._context._id_to_obj.keys():
  size += sys.getsizeof(self.scheduler._context._id_to_obj[k])
```

This returns around 68MB of size.

For _handles, it is around 461MB.

@benjaoming @rtibbles @indirectlylit, the main reason for a session log out was that, when a student does not log out on a particular tablet and the next learner gets the same tablet in the next session then he/she is logged in to the previous leaners account. 

But like @benjaoming pointed out, there are use cases when a teacher would request the learners to pause and explain a concept or a child might pause from his current work, help his/her friends to clear some doubts.

Since the logout uses case cannot be implemented when a learner closes the browser or when a tablet is turned off, it might be worth while to keep 10 minutes as a logout time rather than 5 minutes. 
Reason being:
* Even if the leaners are sharing the same tablets for the next session, it takes more than 10 minutes before they use the same tablets again in a different tabs. 
* 10 minutes seams to be a good time before the learner gets back to his or her screen and continue where they paused. If this time exceeds 10 mins its okey for the leaner to be logged out, but there has to be an automatic redirection where in the leaner is logged out and is given the home page clearly indicating that he needs to log out. In todays testing, the 5 minutes session time out implemented on 0.4.6 does not happen smoothly and the leaner is shown an error page, rather than logging out and shown the homepage that would indicate that the session has times out. @swapnil106111 will be logging a separate issues to highlight error sate while the tablet is kept idle for 5 minutes.

Hey Jon,

I see the issue. We recently replaced the underlying SOAP implementation that the client library uses, and the new one is a bit pickier than the old one :). If you look at the ref docs for [ExpandedTextAd](https://developers.google.com/adwords/api/docs/reference/v201710/AdGroupAdService.ExpandedTextAd), you'll notice that fields such as `headlinePart1` and `description` are Strings, not lists. Previous versions of the library would let this slide, but our new one is correctly throwing an error. If you line up your parameters with the types in the ref docs, you'll be all set!

Best,
Gabe

@dfhkvs<https://github.com/dfhkvs> I'll update the docs to reflect this, but a much better option is to $ pip install planet --user. This installs the package within a subdirectory in your home directory and avoids a bunch of potential collisions and frustrations. The one downside is that executables are placed in a directory that is probably not already on your path.

Packages are installed here:

~/Library/Python/$VERSION/lib/python/site-packages/

Why is this better?

Using $ pip install --user is superior in a number of ways:

  1.  The most important reason: $ sudo pip install can execute arbitrary code as root. If a package cannot be installed as a wheel<https://pythonwheels.com> the installation process executes setup.py directly, which means that $ sudo pip install is the same as $ sudo python setup.py for source distributions. Incidentally, we neglected to upload a Python 2 wheel for the most recent release of this package, so a $ sudo pip install planet at the very least triggers this package's setup.py to be executed as root.
  2.  Using $ sudo pip install installs the package globally and can introduce really heinous conflicts between different versions of pip, python, and other users.
  3.  Installing packages in individual virtual environment is a great method for isolating specific workflows, working around conflicting dependencies, or developing new code. In this case it probably makes the $ planet CLI a bit harder to use.

Cool. What do I do now?

I think your virtual environment install is incorrect because you installed planet within the virtual environment as sudo. Unless you have a specific reason for isolating the package within a virtual environment I would recommend uninstalling all possible copies of planet, deleting the virtual environment you created, and then installing with the --user flag. Here are the steps:

  1.  Re-activate the virtual environment: $ source env/bin/activate
  2.  Uninstall the sudo installed version of planet: $ sudo pip uninstall planet
  3.  Make sure no other versions of planet are installed in the environment: $ pip uninstall planet
  4.  Deactivate the environment: $ deactivate
  5.  Delete the environment.
  6.  Uninstall any lingering global sudo installed versions of planet: $ sudo pip uninstall planet
  7.  Uninstall any other lingering planet installations: $ pip uninstall planet
  8.  Install planet for your user only: $ pip install planet --user

If for some reason you do want to keep planet isolated within its own virtual environment, perform steps 1 - 5 and then:

  1.  Create a new environment: $ python -m virtualenv env
  2.  Activate the environment: $ source env/bin/activate
  3.  Ensure the packaging related tools contained in the environment are up to date: $ pip install pip setuptools wheel --upgrade
  4.  Install planet within the environment: $ pip install planet

* when you specify a wrong pattern, no indication -> added errorhandling
* when you specify a "complete" expression that results in direct values, the parsing that expects a key for the domain generates an exception -> always include a wildcard in  your pattern, even if you specify it completely (that way, the path is repeated and the parsing works fine)
* when some attributes are not present in some of the instances, the complete request fails -> added a flag (cfr http://jolokia.963608.n3.nabble.com/MemoryPool-java-lang-UnsupportedOperationException-Usage-threshold-is-not-supported-td3101873.html)

I don't think caching on Travis-CI would be recommended. To quote the [Travis-CI docs](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/caching/#Things-not-to-cache):

> Large files that are quick to install but slow to download do not benefit from caching, as they take as long to download from the cache as from the original source

"Quick to install but slow to download" seems like an accurate description of conda packages. Note the Travis-CI cache is backed by... S3.

I think it's not so rare as you might think.  It is common for many libraries to have optional support for various features, depending on the dependencies available.

A couple examples:

- libtiff can be compiled with optional jpeg support.
- VTK-5 can optionally be built with support for Python, Qt, or both (via PyQt)

As it stands right now, the Anaconda version of those packages don't include those features.  Therefore, I have my own versions, but they are (1) on my own channel, (2) have the same package name as the Anaconda version and (3) have extra dependencies. Bingo!

At this point, I think it's pretty clear that what we _thought_ was the solution to all of our problems, i.e. flipping the `featureless_minimization_disabled_feature_flag` flag, is in fact not the solution to all of our problems.

I therefore want to take a step back and make sure we actually understand the problem. I want to disentangle incorrect metadata from incorrect solver behavior, just so that we can confirm that what we believe to be _correct_ metadata is baseline okay.

I've created https://conda-static.anaconda.org/conda-forge, that has patched metadata as described by https://conda-static.anaconda.org/conda-forge/linux-64/patch_instructions.json.  To test out all of the use cases and workflows we're referencing in this issue, use

    conda config --set channel_alias https://conda-static.anaconda.org

and make sure it's just `conda-forge` and `defaults` in your channels list for now.  Use any version of conda, and for conda 4.5.9 the `featureless_minimization_disabled_feature_flag` parameter can have the default value, which is `false`.

I'd rather keep discussions here in the spirit of conda being an open source project that external people can follow.

Anaconda.org and conda are (obviously) in completely different spaces.  Anaconda.org cannot know what platform anyone might ask for.  It thus must support arbitrary ones.  Conda absolutely can know what platform you want, and moreover, I'd assert that it can have one (and only one) at a time, plus noarch.  We don't need to add any extra logic for this - it is already there on the context.  For this reason, I believe that asking to check two requests rather than hard-coding one is more reasonable.  It has much less likelihood to break anyone in transition.

Just now catching up on some of these issues and seeing this.

For activate and deactivate, we have to be particularly careful about operations that will start to take a lot of time.  If we expect users to use activate consistently, it needs to be *fast*.

In 4.4, one of the things I wanted to do, but didn't get done, was add a json file in `conda-meta` that held various information about the environment's current state.  One thing for sure was going to be the topo sort order of all packages installed in that environment.  That field would be actively managed anytime there was a change to the environment.

If packages used the convention that their scripts in the `activate.d` and `deactivate.d` directories were named the same as the package, then it would be pretty straight forward to execute in the correct order based on just the topo-sorted list of packages.

We could go a step further in complexity and start recording in that file the exact order of all scripts to run for activate and deactivate.

I also mentioned somewhere else today that we're going to fairly soon run into issues with only having `.sh` and `.bat` scripts for activate and deactivate.  We're having more and more users of csh variants, fish, and Powershell.  Or people who want to use those shells at least.  Not to mention xonsh, which in 4.4 already has a supported wrapper within conda.

I think it's going to force us to work out a DSL or something similar that lets us manage environment variables in a more shell-agnostic way.

If this is acceptable and the tests pass I'd really like to get it into AD 5.0.0 (and more importantly, Miniconda 4.3.x).

Finally being able to use MSYS2 with conda is fantastic; I wish I'd seen it though a long time ago. There's still a fly in the ointment, a [hack](https://github.com/AnacondaRecipes/python-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/0005-Win32-Ensure-Library-bin-is-in-os.environ-PATH.patch) deep in the bowels of our Python that I hope to get rid off. It's not completely fatal, just annoying.

Actually, the biggest difference if you're still pinned to `4.3.21`... In `4.3.27`, we added `https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main` to the default value of `defaults`. I do not recommend downgrading to 3.5.1.  The linked example was simply using 3.5.1.  The message, as you have seen, is the same.

```
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3/connectionpool.py:770: InsecureRequestWarning: Unverified HTTPS request is being made. Adding certificate verification is strongly advised. See: https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html
InsecureRequestWarning)
```
This is a non-fatal warning.  You likely cannot get rid of it if you are using self-signed certificates, or there are missing intermediate certificates, or any other number of possible permutations.  It simply says, "I'm not verifying the certificate, just so you know," and then it continues on its merry way.  I have no way of knowing how your certificates were generated, or by whom they were signed.  Curator itself only delegates that testing to the `urllib3` python module, and that module says there's something wrong in the certificate chain.  It's that simple.  Perhaps `urllib3` is pickier than Kibana.  I don't know.  Kibana may still be generating errors in the log file related to the certificate chain, but it keeps working anyway.  I don't have that information handy as it's in your environment.

Using `--master-only` is probably not needed in your use case.  It is used when deploying Curator to _all_ nodes in the cluster, but only having it _complete_ the run on the elected master.

Hi @mxplusb 
When you are using `XXXX_by_id` methods, you need to provide the Api version of the resource provider you're targeting.The API version 2017-05-10 is for provider `Microsoft.Resources` for operations like `resource_groups.create_or_update`. I agree that the documentation is not explicit about it.

For you, since you target `Microsoft.Storage`, you should use 2017-06-01. Or you can directly use the storage client:
```python
client = StorageManagementClient(credentials, subscription_id)
for account in client.storage_accounts.list_by_resource_group(resource_group):
   if "pioneertest" not in account.id:
      client.storage_accounts.delete(resource_group, account.name)
```

We’ve thought about this a bit before, and decided against some of these optimizations. The biggest reason is that downloading cached data takes a majority of processing time, and it’s necessary to download it in order to determine if it’s changed. It would save us a small amount of CPU, but probably not much time.

We use the code version as part of the cache key, though we don't attempt to determine what a significant change might be.

I have recently extended the keep time for runs from five days to ten, in the hopes that we can run the batch process twice as often to get new sources onto the front page faster while decreasing the overall time working.

We had a discussion in Slack and @migurski, @iandees, and I all agree we should zip the CSV output and serve `.zip` files instead of `.csv` files. This lets us avoid the whole S3/gzip encoding question. Also allows us to put extra files into the product if we want, maybe a `.vrt` file or some other metadata.

Hmm, that's odd. That error message means that the entry in the database for the comment associated with that cell is missing. I don't know why it wouldn't be there, though. The way it gets added is:

1. When you run `nbgrader assign`, a "skeleton" assignment is added to the database
2. At the beginning of `nbgrader autograde`, a copy of the "skeleton" assignment is made for each student's submission
3. At the end of `nbgrader autograde`, the skeleton is filled in with comments and grades. This is the step that is failing for you.

So presumably something went wrong with step 1 or step 2. It's possible there's something wrong with the skeleton or maybe just the copy. To check if the skeleton is correct, try running:

```
from nbgrader.api import Gradebook
gb = Gradebook("sqlite:///gradebook.db")
cell = gb.find_solution_cell("03_ex1_tf_nfkb_answer", "7_tf_binding_promoters", "week01")
print(cell)
```

If that throws an error then it means the skeleton is incorrect. If it doesn't throw an error it means the copy is probably incorrect. Let me know what happens and we'll go from there!

Most of the py2 failures were because of a real bug. The samefile backport in #1142 didn't work on Python 2 because the stat fields used (st_ino, st_dev) always return 0 on Windows + Python 2, causing the function to return True for any pair of files. It works everywhere else, though. I updated the method to do case-insensitive file comparison plus full stat comparison (times included), and use the stdlib `os.path.samefile` where it's available (everywhere but win+py2).

An alternative would be to make the fill-in same file just do `path == other_path`, which would never give a false-positive, but would disable renaming with only changed case on Windows + Python 2. Since it works correctly on Python 3 due to samefile, I don't have a huge preference either way.

> installing/uninstalling an extension would require a rebuild of WebPack

I think that's not going to work. We cannot require running webpack to be a runtime dependency for using an extension.

I expect that we should be able to deal with this in JupyterLab by making the extension points more clearly defined exported functions / events / observables / etc., Rather than allowing full access to everything in extensions. If we do that, extensions shouldn't need to be able to extend prototypes on Cell, etc. which is the source of the problems we have in the notebook right now (and then the fact that they cannot extend these prototypes becomes a *good* thing).

A summary of the security/sanitization we do and why:

1. untrusted HTML/js must never be displayed without explicit user action
2. Code cells have an obvious action to take: execute
3. Markdown cells *also* have an obvious action to take: render
4. Instead of tracking whether the user has explicitly trusted markdown cells, we treat them as always untrusted, which means sanitizing markdown results, but we can display them even when the notebook is untrusted
5. A dedicated display-data cell would need to have to have the same trust semantics as other cells (explicit trust before display or sanitization)

If we treat markdown cells as we do code cells, that means that an untrusted notebook needs to open with all markdown cells unrendered, and they must be 'executed' manually by the user before they are allowed to render on the page. The same would be true if we had dedicated mimebundle cells.

So the question to me is, really, a user-experience one on which I don't have an informed opinion. Should we:

1. sanitize markdown, in which case we always get to display it (what we do now)
2. allow arbitrary HTML/js in markdown, in which case we cannot display it until it is trusted

There are technically other options, like "display but sanitize only *untrusted* markdown", which is attractive because it will do what people want the most often, but I think it's the most confusing when sanitization of untrusted markdown produces a materially different result, where we have to communicate to users that they must"unrender then re-render the cell to see it how it's meant to be."

For those of you coming from searching around for a solution, this can be done programmatically inside gunicorn hooks. Here's an example gunicorn config that uses gevent to spawn the profiler server, serving profiler views on ports 118001 onward for each gunicorn worker:
```python
import gevent
import gevent.monkey

_worker_count = 0

def pre_fork(server, worker):
    global _worker_count
    _worker_count += 1
    worker.worker_index = _worker_count

def post_fork(server, worker):
    gevent.monkey.patch_select()
    gevent.monkey.patch_socket()
    import socket
    import profiling.tracing
    from profiling.remote.select import SelectProfilingServer

    profiler = profiling.tracing.TracingProfiler(
        timer=profiling.tracing.timers.GreenletTimer()
    )

    profiling_socket = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    profiling_socket.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    profiling_socket.bind(('127.0.0.1', 11800 + worker.worker_index))
    profiling_socket.listen(1)

    profiling_server = SelectProfilingServer(
        profiling_socket,
        profiler,
        interval=5
    )
    gevent.spawn(profiling_server.serve_forever)
    print('Worker {} listening on {}:{} for profiling.'.format(
        worker.pid, *profiling_socket.getsockname()
    ))
    worker.profiler = profiler


def worker_exit(server, worker):
    worker.profiler.stop()
```

If you're not using gevent, the server can be spawned in a thread instead, but controlling the profiler from the server thread is more difficult because profiling's `BackgroundProfiler` profiler wrapper interfere's with gunicorn's USR2 signal handling. You'll have to roll your own profiler wrapper in this case.

@ambushed Does the following work for you?

```python
from autograd.wrap_util import unary_to_nary
from autograd.core import make_vjp
from autograd.extend import vspace
@unary_to_nary
def value_and_jacobian(fun, x):
    """
    Returns a function which computes `fun` and the Jacobian of `fun` with respect to
    positional argument number `argnum`, which must be a scalar or array. Unlike
    `value_and_grad` it is not restricted to scalar-output functions, but also it cannot
    take derivatives with respect to some argument types (like lists or dicts). If the
    input to `fun` has shape (in1, in2, ...) and the output has shape (out1, out2, ...)
    then the Jacobian has shape (out1, out2, ..., in1, in2, ...).
    """
    vjp, ans = make_vjp(fun, x)
    ans_vspace = vspace(ans)
    jacobian_shape = ans_vspace.shape + vspace(x).shape
    grads = map(vjp, ans_vspace.standard_basis())
    return ans, np.reshape(np.stack(grads), jacobian_shape)
```

Yes I think we should consider implementing this behavior as a default. Maybe the interface could involve two `jacobian` functions, one which does this TJP thing (but might raise an error if it encounters a VJP implementation which can't handle broadcasting along leading dimensions, or if it encounters an intermediate value for which we don't know how to prepend leading dimensions) and one which does the current thing (calling the VJP repeatedly). That is, the changes would be

1. we would try to make as many VJPs as possible broadcast along leading dimensions, 
2. there would only be one backward_pass function, and it would handle leading dimensions like the version in this PR,
3. we would add a `jacobian` function, basically the one implemented in this PR in which it tries to call the vjp/tjp function on a Kronecker tensor, possibly with a different name.

Re: performance, `assert_vspace_match` does the same dtype checks that we'd want to add to `assert_vspace_compatible` when we do the equality check, right? Or are they more complicated for TJPs somehow?

The reason I suggested broadcasting along trailing dimensions was that I was thinking in terms of tensordot: that is, just like `np.tensordot(J, V, dims)` would contract the trailing dimensions of J with the leading dimensions of `V`, so too could our JTPs. More precisely, we could have

`make_jtp(f)(x)[0](V)` === `np.tensordot(jacobian(f)(x), V, np.ndim(x))`

hence the trailing dimensions of `V` would come along for the ride. I think this matches how `make_tjp` works, as in

`make_tjp(f)(x)[0](V)` === `np.tensordot(V, jacobian(f)(x), np.ndim(f(x)))`

However, you're right that we could choose a different convention, and just say the contraction works along trailing dimensions of `V`. It seems slightly messier to specify in terms of tensordot, though.

There could be a couple of issues at work here. The most common error I've seen when loading certificates occurs when the certificate files have the wrong file permissions. However, I think you may not be generating your certificates correctly.

When I generate certificates, I use the following OpenSSL commands:
* `openssl genrsa ...` to generate keys (if needed)
* `openssl req ...` to create self-signed CA certificates
* `openssl ca ...` to create certificates signed by the CA certificate
* `openssl x509 ...` to display certificates
Note here that generating your certificates with the right data and extensions will likely require you to set up your own custom OpenSSL configuration file. See the OpenSSL docs for more information on that.

Going back to the steps you listed above, you can ignore steps 1.3 and 1.4. You don't need your key in PKCS #12 format and the server does not care if your certificate is using a DER or PEM encoding.

For your client/server config settings, things are a bit jumbled. You currently only have two certificates, a CA certificate and a client certificate. You need a third certificate between these two, a server certificate that was signed by the CA and then was itself used to sign the client certificate. The server would use the server certificate and key for its `certificate_path` and `key_path` options and the CA certificate for its `ca_path` option. The client would use the client certificate and key for its `certificate_path` and `key_path` options while also using the CA certificate for its `ca_path` option. Without this setup, it's going to be challenging to establish any sort of successful secured connection.

Finally, the GitHub link you posted may or may not provide sufficient instructions for creating your certificates for your use case. The *best* resources here are the OpenSSL docs and tutorials so if you continue to have issues with your certificates, study them.

Hasty notes on a design conversation, will expand this into a DBEP and/or a PR later.

## Slicing and  Chunking

* `data()`, `table()`, and `events()` should each pick up `slice` and `chunks` (or should we call it `chunk_size`?) parameters.
* `table()` will return one DataFrame is `chunks=None` (its current behavior). It will return a generator of DataFrames with length `chunks` if `chunks` is an integer.
* `events()` will return a generator of dictionaries (its current behavior) if `chunks=None`, but a DataFrame of length `chunks` if `chunks` is an integer. Thus, in the case where `chunks` is not `None`, `events()` is the same as `table()`.
* `data()` will return a generator of things that have length `chunks` over the first axis. There will be some brains that figures out which type of thing this should be: numpy and dask arrays will be automatically _stacked_ for convenience. The general case might be a simple `list`.
* The `slice` parameter will accept a `slice` object or a list of positions. (If `chunks > 1` these will be interpreted as _starting_ positions for the chunk. Requests might result in overlapping chunks; that's fine.)
* For now we are only addressing 1D slicing along the Event axis. Slicing along arbitrary data axes is on our roadmap but out of scope for this particular work.

## API Cleanup

* `db.get_*` methods should be deprecated, vastly reducing the surface area of `Broker` API
* Headers never need to call back to their header source of origin, only event sources. Therefore, `db` should give Header only its _event sources_ , not itself. (So, remove `Header.db`.)
* The configuration file should list the handler registries per event source, so that an event source can know exactly what its specific handlers are.
* Resource and Datum are considered part of an Event Source. (Why? You can't interpret a Datum without a Descriptor and a Resource.)

## Better handler configuration

* The fetching methods (events, table, data, documents, ???) should accept a `handlers` argument with a custom handler registry.
* Usages like this allow the user to explicitly destroy a registry (and the potentially heavy cache therein):

  ```py
  with handler_registry as r:
      header.data('thing', handlers=r)
  ```
* If the `handlers` parameter is not given, the Header will fall back to the Broker's "default" handler registry with an LRU cache. (Does this mean Headers need a reference to `db` after all?)

> I don't see situation where we have `synchronous=False`

Let's imagine that there's a user who wants to make a Satellite start syncing a repository. The user wants to script this, and they prefer to use the API over the CLI (maybe they don't have SSH access to the server?). The user could write a script like this:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from nailgun.entities import Repository
Repository(name='...').search()[0].sync()
```

Great, right? Oh, no, that doesn't work, because the script will hang in perpetuity waiting for the server to respond. They need to write something like this:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from nailgun.entities import Repository
Repository(name='...').search()[0].sync(synchronous=False)
```

There are some details on the pypi/github page (https://github.com/ndevox/elasticsearch2-haystack)

Essentially:

- Pip install the package (`pip install elasticsearch2-haystack`).
- Check which elasticsearch version you are running.
- Make sure you install the version of elasticsearch-py which matches the version of elasticsearch you are using.
- Add the engine to your `HAYSTACK_CONNECTION` setting like so:
```
HAYSTACK_CONNECTIONS = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'elasticsearch2_backend.ElasticsearchSearchEngine',
        'URL': 'http://127.0.0.1:9200/',
        'INDEX_NAME': 'haystack',
    },
}
```

I think the way dispatch stores views right now is just as a count on each article that gets incremented on every view (`dispatch.apps.content.Publishable.views`) -- we're not storing timestamp info so we can't break it down like that currently.

To get things like "most views for the week" we either have to
- write something to periodically pull this stuff out of google analytics (possibly sketchy) or 
- do something like move the `views` count on `Publishable` to be a `View` model that just has a `timestamp` field plus a FK back to the `Publishable` and then whatever other fields we want to record

I've been giving it a try. The transition of the plugin into plugin objects is relatively easy.

However, I noticed the `register_*_helper` interface in the core. I didn't realized before it was not limited to plugins. Like, anyone can register a function in there from his code without a plugin file.

If we move to plugin objects, and we store the spec in the plugin objects, we don't want to pass the spec object when calling the helper methods. This is one of the benefits of the change.

But then this breaks custom non-plugin helpers passed directly through the `register_*_helpers` interface as they don't receive the spec object anymore. This means that those should helpers be passed as plugin objects as well. This could be a bit of an overhead, but not a very big deal.

And there's the specific case of response_helpers. The register function expects a method and a status code. It is currently not used in plugins provided with apispec, but I suppose you added them because you had a use case for them. The interface could be changed to pass the method and the status code to a plugin object reponse_helper method.

```python
class MyApispecPlugin:

    def response_helper(self, method, status_code, **kwargs):
        """One response helper function with multiple cases"""
```

I've overwritten the unwrap_item function for my `RelationshipSchema`s to work with Schemas that don't have attributes and need to know the IDs. @ZeeD26 I think this would work for your situation:

```python
class MyFirstSchema(Schema):
    id = fields.Int()

    class Meta:
        type_ = 'firsts'

    def unwrap_item(self, item):
        """ Overload this function from marshmallow-jsonapi so it actually stores the id. """
        # Work around for ma-jsonapi requiring attributes when loading data.
        if 'attributes' not in item:
            item['attributes'] = {}
        payload = super().unwrap_item(item)
        # Work around for ma-jsonapi not loading the ids
        payload['id'] = item['id']
        return payload
```

'#set custom divisor'

 _buf[6] = buf[7] / baudrate_

_type(buf[6])_ is int, whereas _type(buf[7] / baudrate)_ is type float and this gives an exception in python3.4

To cure I have replaced it by 
_buf[6] = buf[7] // baudrate_

Or even better precision can be obtained by rounding instead. Like this
_buf[6] = (2*buf[7]+baudrate) // baudrate_

I removed the ``gti_dirint.ipynb`` tutorial from this pull request and put it in this [gist](https://gist.github.com/wholmgren/fe4d72ebb2c0e6481680bee5d9041b06). The latest version explores why the ``gti_dirint`` function seems to yield bad results for AOI > 80 (roughly speaking) in some conditions. In short, the iterative algorithm is doing what it's supposed to do: minimize the difference between the modeled and measured POA global. However, this can lead to a less accurate modeled GHI/DNI/DHI at higher AOI. Refactoring the code has made me more confident in the implementation, but that doesn't guarantee it's correct.

* my personal recommendation would be to use [HDF5](https://www.hdfgroup.org/) and [`h5py`](http://www.h5py.org/) but netCDF is also good. AFAICT, XArray is an in-memory data structure, not a serialized format, but [they recommend storing XArray as netCDF](https://xarray.pydata.org/en/stable/io.html#netcdf). Another in-memory data structure is [Apache Arrow](https://arrow.apache.org/) which serializes as [Feather](http://wesmckinney.com/blog/feather-arrow-future/). There are probably many more, and many deciding factors that could influence your choice: maturity, performance, etc.

* For an example of using netCDF4 with NumPy in Python see [`ecmwf_macc_tools.py` in my PVSC-44 repo](https://github.com/mikofski/pvsc44-clearsky-aod/blob/master/pvsc44_clearsky_aod/ecmwf_macc_tools.py) which is applied to AOD data downloaded from the ECMWF API with the [`all_ecmwf_data.py`](https://github.com/mikofski/pvsc44-clearsky-aod/blob/master/all_ecmwf_macc.py) script, once you've registered and installed their [python API client](https://software.ecmwf.int/wiki/display/WEBAPI/Web-API+Downloads). See [How to retrieve ECMWF Public Datasets](https://software.ecmwf.int/wiki/display/WEBAPI/How+to+retrieve+ECMWF+Public+Datasets). You can see how I use the `Atmosphere` class in the [ECMWF section of the Jupyter notebook](http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/mikofski/pvsc44-clearsky-aod/blob/master/PVSC44%20Clearsky%20AOD%20Analysis.ipynb#ECMWF).

* as far as notation I'm in favor of syncing the PVPMC and PVLib notation as much as possible and using verbose names, ie: spell them out, as @wholmgren seems to indicate, but syncing later with OrangeButton and other constituents (CF, Unidata, etc.) will still be an oustanding issue. My advice is to document as much as possible, maybe start with that first, and make the documentation public.

* one thing to note about heirarchical formats is that they are optimized to be sliced along certain dimensions, and will slow down in other directions so understanding how you want to _use_ your data will instruct how you _store_ it.

* re: @cwhanse comment
  >instead of 2 numpy.arrays, what about an array of tuples (v_V, I_A)? The voltage and current come in pairs. It may be awkward to work with them as tuple type, though.

  you can also use [NumPy structured arrays](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/user/basics.rec.html) with nested fields of arbitrary dimensions.

```python
import numpy as np
import pvlib

x = pvlib.pvsystem.singlediode(6.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026, 100)
y = pvlib.pvsystem.singlediode(5.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026, 100)

my_dtype = np.dtype([
  ('i_l', float), ('i_0', float), ('r_s', float), ('r_sh', float), ('nNsVth', float),
  ('i_sc', float), ('v_oc', float), ('i_mp', float), ('v_mp', float),
  ('i', float, (1,100)), ('v', float, (1, 100))
])
my_data = np.array([
    (6.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026, x['i_sc'], x['v_oc'], x['i_mp'], x['v_mp'], x['i'], x['v']),
    (5.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026, y['i_sc'], y['v_oc'], y['i_mp'], y['v_mp'], y['i'], y['v'])
], my_dtype)

my_data['i_l']  # list of all photogenerated currents (`I_L`)
# array([6.1, 5.1])

my_data['i'][0]
# list of cell currents for first record
```python

  I'm not endorsing this, just making sure you all are aware of it. But note how you can make the cell current and voltage fields 1x100 since we know we'll set `ivcurve_pnts` to 100, we know this size. Also we are not likely to _ever_ need to slice across this set of values, so it's okay that they are lumped in a field. Finally, you could just as easily make this entire array M by N dimensions, where, M is the number of temperatures (T), and N is the number of irradiances (E). Then to get all of the I-V curves for a particular (E, T) combination, just use `my_data[m, n]` where `m` is the index of the desired T, and `n` is the index of the desired E. Then you can do NumPy "fancy" indexing to get the i-v curve of at (E, T)

```python
your_data = np.copy(my_data)
# reshape my_data and your_data from (2,) to (1, 2), and concatentate
# you could also use np.atleast_2d or np.tile probably, ...
# lots of options here, not sure best ...
all_data = np.concatenate([my_data.reshape(1,2), your_data.reshape(1,2)], axis=0)
all_data.shape
# (2, 2)
# now "fancy" indexing to get i-v curve at (E, T)
all_data[1,1][['i', 'v']]
([[5.09950248e+00, 5.09674358e+00, 5.09398468e+00, 5.09122577e+00, 5.08846685e+00, ...]],
 [[ 0.        ,  0.339375  ,  0.67875   ,  1.01812499,  1.35749999, ...]])
```

* also you can index into a dictionary using tuples, since they are immutable and therefore hashable, you can use any hashable index for a dictionary

After some days I got a class that collect `code-block` directives and concatenate them respecting document order specified in the toctree of index.

The class add callbacks on the `doctree-resolved` signal for collect code blocks and onde instance of toctree, and on the `build-finished` to wait until all documents be collected and then concatenate blocks to get a full program compiled from all code blocks. The expression `doctree.traverse(lambda node: node.tagname == 'toctree')` and similar are used to find relevant elements in the documents.

The `code-block` directives are inside `only` directives that displays only python or bash code examples depending on `sphinx-build -t tagname` option.

I.e. all code blocks (only bash or python) get compiled into a program that can be executed after documentation was built. The runnable documentation will continue from there.

To get a better understanding of the effect of solver2_timeout on different types of queries, I performed an experiment with a SMT2 query in QF_BV theory (query_bv.smt2). I ran 6 versions as follows:

A) Exact file, i.e. no (push), no (get-unsat-core).
B) Line 1031 uncommented, i.e. with (push)
C) Lines 3 & 1033 uncommented, i.e. with (get-unsat-core)
D) Same as B) with combined_solver.solver2_timeout=5000 (millisec)
E) Same as C) with combined_solver.solver2_timeout=5000 (millisec)
F) Same as E) but with if on lines [219-221](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/2b0106c199a40500bb661c1afdf7818139ccd1d0/src/solver/combined_solver.cpp#L219-L221) in file [solver/combined_solver.cpp](z3/src/solver/combined_solver.cpp) changed to `if (m_ignore_solver1)  {` (i.e. forcing usage of solver1 after solver2 timeout)

Time corresponding to a configuration were as follows:
A -> 0.03 sec
B -> 151.32 sec
C -> 195.48 sec
D -> 5.07 sec
E -> 194.78 sec
F -> 54.18 sec

It seems to me that currently solver2_timeout does not have any influence when parameter produce-unsat-cores is true. Using non-incremental solver queries do seem to be beneficial (or more precisely, preprocessings deployed in non-incremental solver). It might be useful to have a fallback mechanism to a non-incremental solver when querying unsat cores. This may also be related to issue [501](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/501).

Does the above make sense to try further exploration? I believe that getting an unsat-core should not take time more than **time**(config A) * **N** (N = assumptions.size()), since a MUS can be obtained in this time by using N non-incremental queries using a regular deletion based algorithm.

Attachment:
[query_bv.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1140299/query_bv.smt2.txt)

Note - Printing [get_num_assumptions()](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/2b0106c199a40500bb661c1afdf7818139ccd1d0/src/solver/combined_solver.cpp#L219) for this query gives 24 (12 + 12)

There is no support for symbolic regular expressions. You can inline the equations as definitions and Z3 will understand (in this case just do ground evaluation).

     (define-fun guid-re () (RegEx String) 
       (let ((hexdigit (re.union (re.range "A" "F") (re.range "0" "9"))))
          (let ((first ((_ re.loop 8 8) hexdigit))
              (second ((_ re.loop 4 4) hexdigit))
              (third ((_ re.loop 4 4) hexdigit))
              (fourth ((_ re.loop 4 4) hexdigit))
              (fifth ((_ re.loop 12 12) hexdigit))
              (dash (str.to.re "-")))
        (re.++ first dash second dash third dash fourth dash fifth))))

    (define-fun guid-str () String "21EC2020-3AEA-4069-A2DD-08002B30309D")

    (assert (not (str.in.re "asdfghjk" guid-re)))

    (assert (str.in.re guid-str guid-re))
    (check-sat)

It would be easy for us to change the implementation in `get_output_shape_for()`, but the cuDNN implementation requires 4D or 5D tensors. The easiest way to remember is to run `git rebase --interactive master`. You'll be greeted with a text editor listing all your commits and giving some guidance below. You'll have to leave the first commit as it is (unless you want to reword it, using `r`). All the other commits can be changed to `f` (for fixup). Hello,

Don't know if the question is still actual, but I'll try to answer it anyway.

If I understand correctly, you're interested in how much of the code in binary could be covered with the CFG analysis in a specified amount of time. If so, the one possible way to know it is to use the `progress_callback` and `show_progress` keyword arguments of *CFGFast* or *CFGAccurate*, as specified in the docstring:

```
class CFGFast(ForwardAnalysis, CFGBase): 
    ....
    def __init__(self, ...):
        """
        ...
        :param progress_callback:       Specify a callback function to get the progress during CFG recovery.
        :param bool show_progressbar:   Should CFGFast show a progressbar during CFG recovery or not.
        :return: None
        """

The `progress_callback` should be a function, that accepts a single positional argument called `percentage`, which indicates the percentage of analysis completion. The `percentage` value could be used as a metric of CFG coverage.

Please, note, that the *CFG coverage* estimation is rather inaccurate and greatly depends on the exact binary you're working on.

This is a use case I had not considered but after poking around the behavior is, surprisingly, correct.  You're on the right track with your point about entry point names matching the objects they reference.  The string `plugin=root_plugin.cli:main_group` is a set of instructions for `pkg_resources`.  `plugin` is an ID you are assigning to `root_plugin.cli:main_group`, which points to a Python object.  In code terms:

```
from pkg_resources import iter_entry_points

mapping = {e.name: e.load() for e in iter_entry_points('root.plugins')}
```

which produces:

```
{'plugin': <click.core.Group at 0x10b379668>}
```

`click` then uses the `Group()`'s name as the subcommand name in the CLI:

```
mapping['plugin'].name
```

which defaults to the function name, which in this case is `main_group`.

**The solution is to do `@click.group(name='plugin-name')` so you can reference `$ root plugin-name`.**

Alternatively, click derives a default `Group.name` from the decorated function's name, so you could change the name of `main_group()` to `plugin()`.  The `$ foo` command is correct because you're telling `setuptools` to install an executable called `foo` pointing to `main_group`.  For the top `Group()` in the chain `click` uses the executable's name rather than `Group.name`.  You can see this behavior by adding this snippet to your `root_plugin/cli.py` file and executing directly:

```
if __name__ == '__main__':
    main_group()
```

Although the displayed program name can also be changed in this case with `main_group(prog_name='foo')`.

Caveat: if an exception is encountered in `EntryPoint.load()` the plugin will be registered under `EntryPoint.name` rather than `Group.name`.  Executing `$ root plugin` will reveal the traceback.

```
  plugin     † Warning: could not load plugin. See `root plugin --help`.
```

I'll leave this ticket open and add this info to the docs.

Well there is not much info in docs about doing this but as a start (the way it worked for me maybe there is a better way) you need to create a CustomDashboard

http://jet.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dashboard_getting_started.html#set-up-custom-dashboard

Next, you need to extend jet.dashboard.modules.DashboardModule and create your module class, see the original source for have an idea of how it works, basically you need to edit template and render method:

Example:

```
class ModuloUsuariosPorCarrera(modules.DashboardModule):
    template = 'admin/modulos/usuarios_por_carrera.html'
    title = 'Usuarios por carrera'

    def render(self):
        ... your custom method...

```

And next, add it to your custom dashboard

```
def init_with_context(self, context):
        ... other modules here...
        self.available_children.append(ModuloUsuariosPorCarrera)
        ...
```

So this code only makes a number of calls equal to the length of your match history divided by 100 (plus a few extra for static data from ddragon). I have 2600 games played, so your code made ~ 26 calls for me.

That's not enough to get you blacklisted, even if you do this for a few other users. Also, we're almost positive our rate limiter is good and won't ever get you blacklisted. We respect all the rate limits that Riot has, and I've personally used it to make tens of thousands of calls over multiple days, so I'm pretty confident that Cass can't get you blacklisted.

Cass may "pause" to wait for enough time to prevent you from getting blacklisted, which can sometimes take 2-5 minutes (and sometimes longer) depending on your limits. If you got blacklisted, there has to be another script that's using your API key -- that's the only thing I can think of.

Hello @pavloblindnology , 
The values you obtain by modifying this control are not disparity but depth (Z16) values with a different multiplier. 
There is a simple test - disparity is intrinsically in inverse proportion to depth, i.e as depth values increase disparity decreases and vice versa.
Using realsense-viewer it is possible to check that with `disparityMode=1` this is not the case. (values change proportionally and not inverse-proportionally)

Can you share a link to the documentation you used ? - it is possible that it was misleading and we need to update it. 
Thanks you

@shyuep is there any reason for VaspInputSet to not serialize the config dict?

Here is my understanding of the situation based on @matk86 and talking to @montoyjh 

* If you re-load a VaspInputSet class using as_dict and then from_dict, the "config_dict" parameter is lost in that procedure. *It would seem to me that config_dict should be retained during this procedure.*
* The TransmuterSet performs this dump/restore because of the fact that the VaspInputSet is designed to be tied to a particular structure. The VaspInputSet the user provides is for the initial structure. After transmuting the structure into a new structure, a new VaspInputSet must be createad for that new structure. To get this new VaspInputSet, the TransmuterFW is doing the dump/restore of the original vaspinputset but with the structure replaced. Unfortunately, config_dict is lost in this process. @montoyjh and I think it would be better if VaspInputSets didn't require a structure for initialization, only when calling the get_inputs function.

Although we can probably patch this in TransmuterFW, e.g., by manually copying the config_dict after dump/restore, it's probably better fixed in pymatgen. What do you think?

```
query1 = '''
    query Bad($foo: Float) {
        hello(foo: $foo)
    }

result = graphql(schema, query1, variable_values={'foo': 3})

```

The `tensorflow.summary` module probably won't be what you're looking for: those ops are for writing summaries, but you already have an event file. There is a `summary_iterator` function in `summary_iterator.py`, but this provides a pretty low-level API.

It sounds like you primarily want to analyze the graph. TensorBoard is well equipped to do this. Use the `tensorboard.backend.event_processing.event_multiplexer` module. Here is an example:

```py
from tensorboard.backend.event_processing import event_multiplexer

LOGDIR = '/home/wchargin/data/scalars_demo/'
RUN_NAME = 'temperature:t0=270,tA=270,kH=0.001'

def main():
  multiplexer = event_multiplexer.EventMultiplexer()
  multiplexer.AddRunsFromDirectory(LOGDIR)
  multiplexer.Reload()

  graph = multiplexer.Graph(RUN_NAME)
  print(len(graph.node))
  print(graph.node[0])

if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

Yes, the intent is to allow `MeshForwarder` to change the outgoing packet as needed.  Couple comments:
1. Indirect Transmissions should always take priority.  For example, if a Data Request frame is received and a frame is pending, we should try to service that immediately.  I believe this PR does not support this behavior.
2. After receiving a Data Request from a child and scheduling an Indirect Transmission for that child, we should stay committed to sending an Indirect Transmission to that child.  This PR will help address this scenario.  At the same time, I think the root cause was introduced in #1646.  Because we update `mStartChildIndex` in `ScheduleTransmissionTask()`, then next time `ScheduleTransmissionTask()` is called, things can change.

Does the issue you described above only occur when we receive multiple Data Request messages from different Children?  If so, what if we simply move the update of `mStartChildIndex` until after we have completed the Indirect Transmission operation?

I think it's also worth noting that a multilabel model has no way of telling you that none of the labels are appropriate for a certain document.
Say we have three labels: mathematics, biology, computing. Some of our ground truth data has one of the labels, some has two, some has all three, and some has none of them. At prediction time, the probability mass of 1.0 will always be distributed across the three labels, but a document about art should probably not have any of the three labels. Prediction `@k` does not help here, because we would have to know the correct `k` for each document.
Training separate binary models (mathematics vs. non-mathematics, biology vs. non-biology and computing vs. non-computing) is a way to deal with this, as suggested by @adam2326.

I agree that it would be nice *at least* to mark a function as disallowing an implicit bytes ->(utf8)-> std::string.  (Here "bytes" and "str" have their Py3 meanings.)  An example case would be pathnames: if python passes in a str, we want to encode it using the filesystem encoding (not necessarily utf-8), if python passes in a bytes, we should assume os.fsencode() has already been called on it and just pass it accordingly.  If pybind11 always does the case, I believe we can't distinguish between the two cases (other than taking a py::object as argument and typechecking ourselves).

I weakly tried this before writing to you, if I remember well. The problem is that I don't have a default constructor for the underlying type. I thought at some point that the pybind11 mechanism might call the `shared_ptr` with `nullptr`, but it is actually looking for the default constructor of the underlying type. 

But now I figured that I am just not handling `std::shared_ptr` and `std::unique_ptr` semantics well, so I did a bit of digging and the following lines now seem to work:

```
std::unique_ptr<std::shared_ptr<monty::ndarray<double, 2>>> ptr_ptr_nda = std::make_unique<std::shared_ptr<monty::ndarray<double, 2>>>(std::make_shared<monty::ndarray<double, 2>>(ptr, shape));
value_ptr.reset(ptr_ptr_nda.release());
```
Before, I guess I was resetting the `std::unique_ptr` with a naked pointer to the underlying `std::shared_ptr`, which is kind of nasty and the `std:shared_ptr` would ref count to 0 and be done. Now I am actually creating a `std::unique_ptr`, then am transferring ownership properly, releasing the old one and resetting the new one.

With respect to the cmake_example repo, the gist of it would be something like:

1. mv src/ to cmake_example/src.
2. In setup.py, add packages=['cmake_example'] to the setup() call.
3. Edit the CmakeLists.txt to refer to cmake_example/src instead of src.

This will compile all modules into cmake_example/*.so and a "python3 setup.py install" will install those modules along with any Python files in cmake_examples/.

With the Certbot name transition complete, and many other parts of the client looking stable, the core team is going to be turning its attention to Nginx next.  Which means sorting out this parser question is probably our top priority.

For my research, I've started by [looking at how Nginx does its own parsing](https://github.com/nginx/nginx/blob/master/src/core/ngx_conf_file.c#L101). It uses a hand-rolled explicit state machine, which is going to make verifying the grammar hard.  But it's something of an argument for landing @olabini 's parser, since we could potentially walk through the state machine code and ensure the two match?

Some other obvious options are (1) complicate our pyparsing to explicitly parse and prserve the whitespace as a part of the grammar; (2) switch to augeas.

Hi @joohoi , thanks for your feedback!

Simply overriding the target name was an option I did consider. I opted for doing CNAME chain resolution instead out of a DRY consideration: requiring the user to provide the target of the CNAME repeats some information we know already exists in the DNS. Also, one thing to keep in mind is that we need to handle the case of multiple subjectaltnames being requested: in that case you have different target names, so the command line option to override the challenge domain needs to be per-subjectaltname (just like -w is for webroot).

On the other hand, I do see the merit of not dragging in an additional dependency.

So, I'll send in a modified version:*

- removing the CNAME chain resolution
- adding a new option allowing to set a per-domain override for the challenge domain
- in order to make it more convenient and limit the amount of stuff that needs to be repeated, I'll make it a format string so that you can say:

`--dns-challenge-domain "{domain}.mychallenges.example.com" -d domain1.example.com -d domain2.example.com`

instead of having to write:

`--dns-challenge-domain domain1.example.com.mychallenges.example.com -d domain1.example.com --dns-challenge-domain domain2.example.com.mychallenges.example.com -d domain2.example.com`

(However I think the second, more verbose form needs to be supported anyway, so I'll steal some code from webroot -- I'll try to see if some of it can be shared).

@Randl I don't think this is necessary. We can do as @alykhantejani said and use two datasets, one for training and one for testing. There is a simple functionality that allows us to split a dataset: [`Subset` and `random_split`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/utils/data/dataset.py#L93-L118)
So that's what you'd need to do
```python
base_dataset = MyDataset(...)
from torch.utils.data.dataset import random_split
train_size, test_size = len(dataset) - 100, 100
train_dataset, test_dataset = random_split(base_dataset, [train_size, test_size])
```

And if you need different transforms for train/test, you can do something like
```python
train_dataset = MyDataset(..., transform=train_transform)
val_dataset = MyDataset(..., transform=val_transform)

from torch.utils.data.dataset import Subset

indices = torch.randperm(len(train_dataset))
train_indices = indices[:-100]
val_indices = indices[-100:]

train_dataset = Subset(train_dataset, train_indices)
val_dataset = Subset(val_dataset, val_indices)
```

@pgavlin, @ellismg, @lukehoban, We talked about it briefly, but I don't remember where we landed: What do we do for templates/examples/apps that require an intermediate build step before it can be deployed? In general, the best practice will be for these to be deployable, but there will be cases that require a build step (e.g. go templates).

Some ideas:

- We could add a property that could be specified in `Pulumi.yaml` under `template:` to opt-out of automatic `up`. Or opt-in.

- We could ask the language host if we should proceed with an automatic `up`. For go, the language host could say "no". For nodejs, we could do some analysis to see if there is a build script specified in `package.json` (for old style TypeScript, or other transpile scenarios).

 - I don't love this as it's not a great experience, but we could just proceed with trying the `up` and let it fail (with good error message on next steps). We'll need the good error message anyway as someone might try to run `up` before the build step anyway when doing things manually.

Yes, @robertnishihara . Current actor resource management is [a lifetime resource](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/src/ray/raylet/node_manager.cc#L1026-L1030), which means once an actor is created, it will occupy the resource until this actor is [disconnected](DisconnectClient). However, this disconnect message will be sent once the instance of an `ActorHandle` is garbage collected ([This code snippet](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/actor.py#L863-L875) shows that only in `__del__` of `ActorHandle` the `DisconnectClient` message will be send.). And there is no warning when this case happens.

In my understanding, precious resource should not be determined by garbage collection behavior. I think we can provide a `release` function like the close function in file handle of python.

Ideally adding a PNG mime type would be the same abstraction at the Widget Manager level.

I got confronted to this trouble in my own project.

The solution I came up with is to wrap the socket used in the tests to catch the `BrokenStreamError` and replace it by a custom exception.

https://github.com/Scille/parsec-cloud/blob/4174ded193c563513c8d7b6aa745112d48986a30/tests/common.py#L45-L63

Then the code responsible to start the server and the connection to it is packaged in an async context manager that will catch the custom BrokenStreamError and wait forever instead of re-raising it.

https://github.com/Scille/parsec-cloud/blob/4174ded193c563513c8d7b6aa745112d48986a30/tests/common.py#L92-L115

This way we can be sure the exception coming from the server will be the one that will bubble up to the user.

Okay, I made a couple more small changes:

* use `logging.debug("stuff %r", msg)` instead of `logging.debug("stuff %r" % msg)`, because it avoids actually formatting the message unless it is being displayed.  I learned this one the hard way in the IPython.parallel hub, where formatting undisplayed debug messages was actually *the* performance bottleneck.
* catch any Exception in parse_request, because there's no reason invalid JSON, etc. should be allowed to raise if otherwise invalid messages are not.
* Minor docstring/comment typos.
